title: Python for Good: Revealing the Philippines Through Data
text:
1. Data skills should connect to real problems - At Data Engineering Pilipinas, we believe learning data tools should help people understand issues that affect society.
2. The Philippines in context - Looking at the Philippines alone can be misleading. - Comparing with regional peers helps us see where we are ahead and where we can improve.
3. Open data + Python - Global organizations publish development data openly. - With Python, anyone can analyze this data transparently and reproducibly.
4. What this notebook demonstrates A simple but real analytics workflow: - collecting public data
- cleaning and reshaping it
- visualizing patterns
- interpreting results responsibly
Goal:
Build a small SDG comparison notebook that shows how the Philippines compares with similar countries.

title: Python for Good: Revealing the Philippines Through Data
text:
1. Data skills should connect to real problems - At Data Engineering Pilipinas, we believe learning data tools should help people understand issues that affect society.
2. The Philippines in context - Looking at the Philippines alone can be misleading. - Comparing with regional peers helps us see where we are ahead and where we can improve.
3. Open data + Python - Global organizations publish development data openly. - With Python, anyone can analyze this data transparently and reproducibly.
4. What this notebook demonstrates A simple but real analytics workflow: - collecting public data
- cleaning and reshaping it
- visualizing patterns
- interpreting results responsibly
Goal:
Build a small SDG comparison notebook that shows how the Philippines compares with similar countries.

title: Python for Good: Revealing the Philippines Through Data
text:
What This Notebook Contains
30-minute guided walkthrough notebook
This notebook is designed for a short live workshop on civic data analysis using open development indicators.

Guiding question
How does the Philippines compare with similar countries on digital development and human capability indicators?


What learners will practice

loading and checking public development data
reshaping data for analysis
comparing countries fairly
exploring relationships between indicators
building a simple composite index
interpreting results responsibly

title: Python for Good: Revealing the Philippines Through Data
text:
0. Setup
We start with imports, configuration, and a few helper mappings.
The notebook assumes your SDG-style indicator extracts already exist in a local data/ folder.
Expected long-format fields - country - country_code - indicator - indicator_code - TIME_PERIOD - OBS_VALUE
If your filenames differ, update DATA_PATH in the loading cell below.
To generate the data file, run your pipeline script (e.g. python run_pipeline.py) before executing this notebook.

Not charted







Indicator
Why excluded




population_total
Used as bubble size in scatter plots only


air_passengers
Raw count — not comparable across country sizes (China/India dwarf others)






A note on data quality

We use the Last Observed Value (LOV) approach — the most recent available data point up to SNAPSHOT_YEAR. Not every country reports every indicator every year. Always check data_age on hover to see how current each value is.

Two indicators to watch closely: - poverty_headcount — Singapore and the US have no national poverty line data (NaN). This is expected: high-income countries use different poverty measures. - education_secondary — The Philippines shows a sharp drop in 2021 (57.88 → 32.43). This likely reflects a methodology or survey change, not a real decline. Treat pre- and post-2020 values with caution when reading the trend chart.

title: Python for Good: Revealing the Philippines Through Data
text:
1. Problem Statement
A common public question is whether the Philippines is “doing well” or “falling behind.”
That sounds simple, but it is actually a comparison problem.
To answer it responsibly, we need to ask:

Compared to whom?
ASEAN peers? Large emerging economies? Advanced economies?
On what indicators?
Connectivity, education, income, longevity, poverty?
In what year?
Not all countries update all indicators at the same time.

So in this notebook we use a small benchmarking framework: - compare the Philippines with a curated set of peers - focus on a few interpretable indicators - inspect missingness before drawing conclusions - use visualizations to explore patterns rather than force a single “winner”

title: Python for Good: Revealing the Philippines Through Data

This cell tries a few common file patterns: - one combined long file in data/curated - multiple per-indicator files in data/curated - fallback to data/raw
Update the filename logic if your project uses a different convention.

For comparison charts, however, it is often easier to work in wide format, where each indicator becomes a column.
We will: - keep only selected countries and indicators - coerce year and value columns - map country groups - pivot to a wide snapshot table

title: Python for Good: Revealing the Philippines Through Data
text:
5. Data Quality Check
Responsible comparison starts with data quality.
We should check: - missing values - latest year available per indicator-country - whether some indicators are less current than others
This matters because a 2023 internet value and a 2019 poverty value are not equally current.

title: Python for Good: Revealing the Philippines Through Data
text:
6. Snapshot Comparison
Now we build our first comparison view: a single-year snapshot.
This is useful for questions like: - Who is ahead on internet access? - Where does the Philippines sit on education or life expectancy? - Are there visible clusters of countries?
We will start with a grouped bar chart for selected indicators.

title: Python for Good: Revealing the Philippines Through Data
text:
7. Scatter Relationships
Bar charts help with direct comparison. Scatter plots help with relationships.
A useful civic question is:

Do countries with stronger digital access also tend to have better human development outcomes?

We will compare: - internet users vs GDP per capita - internet users vs life expectancy - education vs poverty

title: Python for Good: Revealing the Philippines Through Data
text:
8. Clustering View
Without doing advanced clustering algorithms, we can still create a visual “clustering view” by plotting two dimensions and coloring country groups.
This lets learners see rough patterns: - ASEAN peers may occupy similar bands - advanced economies may separate clearly - the Philippines may sit between groups depending on the indicator pair

title: Python for Good: Revealing the Philippines Through Data
text:
9. Trends Over Time
Snapshots are helpful, but they can hide movement.
To understand development, we also want to ask: - Is the Philippines improving? - Is it improving faster or slower than peers? - On which indicators is the gap narrowing?
We will look at time trends from 2000 onward.

title: Python for Good: Revealing the Philippines Through Data
text:
10. Philippines vs Peers
A workshop audience usually wants to focus on the Philippines.
So here we compare the Philippines against: - the ASEAN peer average - the large emerging average - the advanced economy average
This turns many country lines into a clearer benchmarking story.

title: Python for Good: Revealing the Philippines Through Data
text:
11. Ranking Snapshot
Rankings are intuitive for a live audience, but they should be handled carefully.
They are useful for: - getting a quick sense of position - identifying outliers - spotting where the Philippines is relatively strong or weak
They are not the same as a complete development story.

title: Python for Good: Revealing the Philippines Through Data
text:
12. Who Improved Fastest
Instead of only asking “Who is highest?”, we can ask a more dynamic question:

Which countries improved the fastest over time?

That is often more meaningful for development analysis.
We will compute simple change from the first available year to the latest available year.

title: Python for Good: Revealing the Philippines Through Data
text:
13. Correlation Matrix
Correlation does not prove causation, but it helps us explore which indicators move together in this small benchmark set.
With a very small country set, this is descriptive rather than definitive.

title: Python for Good: Revealing the Philippines Through Data
text:
14. Composite Development Index
A composite index can be useful for teaching because it combines several indicators into one simplified view.
But it must be presented honestly: - it depends on indicator choice - it depends on scaling - it reflects analyst judgment
Here we build a simple teaching index: - higher is better for internet, education, GDP per capita, life expectancy - lower is better for poverty, so we reverse it - we use min-max scaling across countries - then take the average

title: Python for Good: Revealing the Philippines Through Data
text:
14b. Context Indicators
These four indicators broaden the picture beyond the core set.
They are shown as a single snapshot chart rather than receiving the full trend/scatter/ranking treatment — the goal is coverage, not deep analysis.







Indicator
Key thing to notice




gini_index
Higher = more unequal. Where does the Philippines sit?


hospital_beds
Physical healthcare capacity per 1,000 people


co2_per_capita
Philippines is low — a strength worth discussing


education_expenditure
Government prioritization of education (% of GDP)




Note: air_passengers is in the dataset but excluded from this chart because it is an absolute count — China and India dwarf everyone else and make the chart unreadable. This is a deliberate visualization choice.

title: Python for Good: Revealing the Philippines Through Data
text:
15. Geographic View
All previous charts show countries as labels or bars.
This choropleth adds spatial context — where are these countries relative to each other, and how does internet access vary across the Asia-Pacific region?
Design choices: - Zoomed to Asia-Pacific (center lat 15, lon 115) so the 9-country set fills the frame - All countries shown — not filtered to ASEAN only — for honest regional comparison - internet_users_pct chosen as the indicator because it has complete coverage across all countries and tells a clear geographic story

title: Python for Good: Revealing the Philippines Through Data
text:
15. Responsible Interpretation

What this notebook does well

creates transparent, reproducible comparisons
shows multiple indicators instead of one headline
encourages learners to inspect missingness and time coverage
helps the audience ask better public questions



What this notebook does not do

prove why one country performs better than another
settle political debates
capture every relevant SDG dimension
guarantee fairness if source data quality differs across countries



Takeaways

Always define the comparison group.
Always inspect the year coverage.
Do not overtrust rankings.
Use multiple views: snapshot, trend, relationship, and index.
Treat composite indicators as a starting point, not the final truth.



Did we answer our question?

Based on these charts, where does the Philippines appear relatively strong, relatively weak, and improving the fastest?

title: Python for Good: Revealing the Philippines Through Data
section: 16. Call to Action: Ask Better Questions
text:
16. Call to Action: Ask Better Questions
Data is already here. Tools are already available.
It’s not perfect — but it’s enough to start.
What matters now is:

What questions are we choosing to ask?


Questions Worth Exploring
Healthcare Can we predict when hospitals will be overwhelmed?
Economy What drives food price spikes — and who gets hit first?
Mobility How much are we losing to traffic — and how can we reduce it?
Digital Access Does internet access actually improve opportunities?
Disaster Risk Which communities are most vulnerable?


Build in Public — Don’t Do This Alone
Start small. Use open data. Use open tools.
Then:

Share your work
Collaborate with others
Learn in public


Progress compounds faster when it’s shared.



Communities You Can Join
Python PH https://www.facebook.com/groups/570524223003384/
Data Engineering Pilipinas https://dataengineering.ph/
R Users Group PH https://www.facebook.com/rugph/


Closing Thought

Data will not change the country. People asking better questions with data will.

Start small. Stay curious. Share what you find.
